# Project plan

## Overview

The project is planned to start in **November 2026** and have a runtime of **10 months**. The Spatial Data Science Across Languages workshop in Fall 2027 (exact dates to be confirmed) will mark the end of the project. The first three WPs are allocated an equal amount of time, 10 weeks each, without overlap. However, after completion of each WP, testing and bug fixing will continue until the CRAN release, planned for Summer 2027. WP4 is spread over the entire project runtime, as documentation of new functionalities will be done alongside code writing. Other dissemination activities in WP4, such as the journal publication and workshop organization, are clustered towards the end of the project. The project plan is visualized graphically in the chart below and explained in more detail in the upcoming sections.

```{r  label = chart, echo = FALSE, fig.cap = "Project plan", out.width = '100%'}
knitr::include_graphics("img/spanner.png")
```

## Start-up phase
<!--
Covering the planning phase, this section should provide a relatively
detailed plan of how work will start on the project. This section is
important because projects need to get up and running quickly.


 - [ ] Setting up collaboration platform inc. code for contributors etc.
 - [ ] License decisions
 - [ ] Reporting framework
-->

The new version of `sfnetworks` builds upon the existing version, using the same collaboration platform, license, etc. First steps in the development of the new version have already been taken and some of the code is available on GitHub already. Therefore, the project does not require a start-up phase, and code writing can start right away.

## Technical delivery
<!--
Covering the actual delivery of the project this section should
provide at least a high-level description of the implementation.

Including target dates is really important as you need to be committed
and the ISC need to have a means of tracking delivery
-->

The main deliverable of the project is the release of version 1.0 of the `sfnetworks` package. First, a beta-version will be released on the GitHub repository of `sfnetworks`. This is planned after completion of the first three WPs, in **June 2027**. The release will be accompanied by corresponding documentation, a changelog outlining how the new version is different from the previous, and an announcement to the user community with a request to test the new code. Once mature, the new version will be submitted to CRAN. This is planned for **July 2027**.

## Other aspects

<!--
How will you ensure that your work is available to the widest number
of people? Please specify the open-source or creative commons license(s)
you will use, how you will host your code so that others can contribute,
and how you will publicize your work. We encourage you to plan content
to be shared quarterly on the R Consortium blog.

 - [ ] Announcement post
 - [ ] Delivery blog post
 - [ ] Social media
 - [ ] UseR!
 - [ ] ISC meetings
-->

Besides the technical delivery, the project aims to disseminate the work to a wide audience. This is achieved through the following deliverables:

1. A blogpost on the r-spatial blog presenting the new CRAN release: **July 2027**
2. An academic article published in the Journal of Statistical Software: **September 2027**
3. A cross-language session on geospatial network analysis at the Spatial Data Science Across Languages workshop: **September 2027**

If requested, we are happpy to also post the blog on the R Consortium blog, and host an online webinar about the new release.

We will continue to use the current GitHub repository of `sfnetworks`: [https://github.com/luukvdmeer/sfnetworks](https://github.com/luukvdmeer/sfnetworks). Here, an active user community is already present. The repository contains an [issue tracker](https://github.com/luukvdmeer/sfnetworks/issues) for bug reports and feature requests, a [discussion forum](https://github.com/luukvdmeer/sfnetworks/discussions), a [code of conduct](https://github.com/luukvdmeer/sfnetworks/blob/main/CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md) and detailed [contributing guidelines](https://github.com/luukvdmeer/sfnetworks/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md). The license of the package will remain unchanged: Apache 2.0. Regarding social media, we will post updates about the development on the personal [Mastodon account](https://datasci.social/@lvdmeer) of Lucas van der Meer, who has developed a good network in the field of open-source geospatial network analysis.

## Budget & funding plan

<!--
Note that the maximum grant is $20k USD. The proposal should be in USD.

Outline the milestones for development and how much funding will be
required for each stage (as payments will be tied to project milestone
completion). This also helps the ISC in the event of partial funding. 
Each milestone should specify the work to be done and the
expected outcomes, providing enough detail for the ISC to understand
the scope of the project work and assess the likelihood of success.

We expect that most of the budget will be allocated
for labor costs. We do not cover indirect costs. The ISC grants cannot
cover such things as travel, lodging, food, journal publication fees,
or personal hardware. Cloud services may be covered if they are specific
to the project and the project period. The ISC reserves the right to vet
how funds are used for each project separately. If in doubt, please reach
out to us.
-->

Lucas van der Meer is currently employed as a research assistant with a 20 h/week contract at the University of Salzburg. He works in research projects that apply geospatial network analysis, but are not specifically focused on software development and maintenance. The idea of this proposal is to be able to increase his working hours by 20%, i.e. 4 h/week on average, that he can dedicate exclusively to the development of `sfnetworks`. Over the total project runtime of 10 months, this translates to a total of 160 hours. Considering his hourly income of 53,20 EUR, it sums to a total funding amount of 8.512 EUR, equivalent (after rounding) to **10.000 USD**. The funding will be split equally across each WP, meaning that every WP will be allocated 2.500 USD. Hence, the cost breakdown is as follows:

- **WP 1:** 2.500 USD (40h)
- **WP 2:** 2.500 USD (40h)
- **WP 3:** 2.500 USD (40h)
- **WP 4:** 2.500 USD (40h)
- **SUM:** 10.000 USD (160h)
